coredata.dc:description: Water pollution is a significant problem in today’s modern world, with dye molecules being the most challenging pollutants to eliminate. Carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated high efficacy in water and wastewater treatment. This research aimed to develop CDs using banana peel as a carbon precursor through a facile hydrothermal method. These CDs were utilized to eliminate methylene blue (MB) dye. The CDs exhibited blue fluorescence with an improved quantum yield (QY) of up to 19 %. They showed an adsorption efficiency of 71.942 mg g−1, with maximum adsorption occurring within a pH range from 2 to 10. The adsorption kinetic data were well described by the pseudo-second-order and Freundlich isotherm models. Thermodynamic studies revealed that the adsorption process was spontaneous and exothermic. Moreover, the CDs demonstrated excellent re-usability potential over five successive cycles of adsorption. Additionally, a prediction study was performed using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to forecast the adsorption of dye by CDs. The CDs were further investigated for cytotoxic properties and cellular internalization in breast cancer cells (MCF-7). The findings indicated that cellular viability significantly decreased with increasing dosage, reaching 50 % (IC50) at a concentration of 128 μg mL−1. Successful internalization of CDs by cancer cells with blue emission suggests their potential in biomedical applications. Thus, the synthesized nanomaterial is highly promising for both bio-sensing applications and wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution is a significant problem in today’s modern world, with dye molecules being the most challenging pollutants to eliminate. Carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated high efficacy in water and wastewater treatment. This research aimed to develop CDs using banana peel as a carbon precursor through a facile hydrothermal method. These CDs were utilized to eliminate methylene blue (MB) dye. The CDs exhibited blue fluorescence with an improved quantum yield (QY) of up to 19 %. They showed an adsorption efficiency of 71.942 mg g−1, with maximum adsorption occurring within a pH range from 2 to 10. The adsorption kinetic data were well described by the pseudo-second-order and Freundlich isotherm models. Thermodynamic studies revealed that the adsorption process was spontaneous and exothermic. Moreover, the CDs demonstrated excellent re-usability potential over five successive cycles of adsorption. Additionally, a prediction study was performed using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to forecast the adsorption of dye by CDs. The CDs were further investigated for cytotoxic properties and cellular internalization in breast cancer cells (MCF-7). The findings indicated that cellular viability significantly decreased with increasing dosage, reaching 50 % (IC50) at a concentration of 128 μg mL−1. Successful internalization of CDs by cancer cells with blue emission suggests their potential in biomedical applications. Thus, the synthesized nanomaterial is highly promising for both bio-sensing applications and wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The industrial revolution, mechanization of agriculture, and advanced technologies have exacerbated global environmental issues [1]. Groundwater is being excessively utilized as a result of urbanization and industrial activity, leading to the discharge of various organic pollutants into the environment and the deterioration of essential water sources for humans and other living organisms [2,3]. The consumption of water containing unsafe contaminants results in diseases and deaths worldwide [4]. Among the different industrial water contaminants, organic dye molecules are recognized as a substantial contributor. Due to their high degree of organic structural complexity, their removal from wastewater effluents is challenging. Developing high-quality, innovative methods and approaches for eliminating dyes from wastewater is vital for achieving the required water quality standards. It is important to note that although numerous conventional techniques have been employed to eliminate pollutants from water and wastewater, none of them are operationally effective in removing dyes from waste effluent [5].The adsorption method has garnered much interest compared to other approaches due to its simplicity, operation feasibility, mechanical flexibility, and low environmental impact [6].

Numerous adsorbent materials, such as zeolite, charcoal, Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), carbon, metal oxides, and layered double hydroxides (LDHs), are employed to eliminate dyes from wastewater. However, their high production costs and low-performance limit their effectiveness as adsorbents [7,8]. In recent years, carbon-based materials have gained considerable attention for treating dye-contaminated water as adsorbent [9,10], as catalyst supports [11], and as energy storage devices [12] due to their exceptional properties including physicochemical stability, superior electrical conductivity, ductility, high surface area, good removal performance, and porous structure [13,14]. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a particularly promising “zero-dimensional” carbon-based material, offering limitless potential in various applications such as medical diagnosis, fluorescent sensing, photocatalysis, and water decontamination. This is attributed to their intrinsic advantages, including outstanding bio-compatibility, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, strong stability, and high specific surface area [15]. Nature provides abundant resources for preparing CDs with unique properties, leading to significant efforts focused on preparing CDs from natural resources due to their eco-friendliness, abundance, cost-effectiveness, ease of preparation, and non-toxicity [16,17]. Moreover, CDs derived from green precursors are rich in various heteroatoms, resulting in abundant surface functional groups with characteristic properties without requiring further passivation or modification [18]. Various synthesis approaches, including top-down and bottom-up methods have been reported for producing CDs [19,20]. Bottom-up methods, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, and ultrasonic methods, are favored over top-down approaches due to their simplicity and environmental friendliness. Among these, the hydrothermal method stands out as a cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and scalable approach [21]. Literature reports suggest the utilization of various biodegradable wastes as precursors for synthesizing CDs through eco-friendly methods to remove various dyes from aqueous solutions [22,23].

The development of mathematical models to estimate the adsorption capacities of sorbent materials plays a crucial role in the effective design of adsorption treatment units. The adsorption mechanism is influenced by diverse environmental factors, including solution pH, adsorbate concentration, adsorbent dosage, and reaction time [24]. Due to the intricate relationship between input variables and experimental outcomes, conventional statistical models cannot accurately predict the percentage of removal in adsorption processes. Consequently, computational models such as random forest (RF), least-square support vector regression (LSVR), and artificial neural networks (ANN) are increasingly employed in the treatment of dye wastewater [25]. These computational models offer advantages such as simplicity, robustness, nonlinearity, reliability, and flexibility compared to statistical models. In particular, ANN has proven to be an effective tool for the precise prediction of adsorbate removal efficiency, utilizing experimental adsorption data as input parameters [26].

In this work, we explored the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from kitchen waste without any passivates or additives, aiming to develop a promising adsorbent for the sequestration of MB and enhance the utilization efficiency of natural resources. We characterized the CDs using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Scanning and Transmission electron microscopy (SEM/TEM), Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis (BET), Ultra Violet-Visible Spectroscopy (UV–vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy. The adsorption behavior of the CDs was examined by determining various influencing parameters, including, pH, adsorbent dosage, time, and initial concentration, using a batch experimental setup. Systematic studies were conducted and discussed to evaluate the kinetics, isotherms, and thermodynamics, providing insights into the adsorption behavior of dye molecules onto the adsorbent. Additionally, one objective of this study was to employ a machine learning algorithm, specifically an artificial neural network, to model the adsorption process of the dye by CDs. Furthermore, the anticancer properties of the synthesized CDs against breast cancer cells were investigated. This study not only introduces an innovative biomass-derived adsorbent but also expands the application of CDs in wastewater treatment and the biomedical field, opening new avenues for sustainable environmental development and providing new insights.

Banana peels were obtained from a local market in Aligarh, India. Absolute ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.9 %) was procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Methylene Blue (C16H18ClN3S, MW: 319.85 g mol−1) was purchased from Merck Chemical Co. No additional purification has been done on any materials and reagents before its utilization.

The morphology of the CDs was confirmed by SEM and EDX analysis (JSM, 6510LV, JEOL, Japan) at USIF, Aligarh Muslim University, India. TEM images were captured using a JEOL-JEM 2100 TEM (Camera-AMT) microscope operated at 200 KV on a carbon-coated copper grid to investigate the size and morphology of CDs. The powder XRD patterns were recorded using a Rigaku X-ray powder diffractometer-PW1148/89 (Netherlands; CuKa radiation). Functional groups of the CDs were analyzed using FTIR (Model Spectrum-BX, USA), and Raman microscopy (Renishaw inVia 2000 system, UK). Zeta potential and UV–vis measurements were obtained with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS at room temperature and a Perkin Elmer LAMBDA-850, respectively. Fluorescence analysis was performed on a Jasco FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The liquid CDs samples were lyophilized using an IIshin Bio Base TFD 8503 lyophilizer. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were recorded on BELSORP-mini-X (Microtrac, USA) at liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K). XPS spectra were obtained using a Thermoscientific, Portland, OR, USA with Kα monochromatic source.

Briefly, 0.5 g of dried banana peels were finely ground and added to a solution of deionized water and ethanol (80 mL). The mixture was then placed in a stainless steel autoclave with Teflon lining for hydrothermal treatment [27]. A mixed cellulose ester membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm was used to filter the precipitated solution, and the clear supernatant was collected. Finally, the prepared fluorescent carbon dot solution was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator until use (Scheme 1 ).

Equation.1 has been used to determine the QY of carbon dots. Typically, quinine sulfate serves as the reference material. Where “Q” stands for the quantum yield; “I” represents the emission intensity of the sample; “A” refers to the absorbance, and “n” denotes the refractive index. The subscript R and CD represents the standard and the CDs, respectively [28].

To perform the adsorption experiments, synthesized CDs were used as an adsorbent, along with MB dye, and a thermostated mechanical shaker. Several experiments were conducted to determine the effect of various variables, such as solution pH, initial sorbent dosages, time, adsorbate concentration, and operating temperature, on the adsorption effectiveness.These experiments also helped identify the interactions between the adsorbate and adsorbent. Initially, 300 mg of CDs were added to a dye solution (20 ppm, 50 mL) in 100 mL iodine flasks, and a visual change in the dye solution's color was observed. After a specific adsorption period, the samples were filtered (filter paper: pore size- 0.45 μm), and the supernatant/ unadsorbed dye was analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (λmax-664 nm) [29]. The adsorption kinetic, equilibrium isotherms and thermodynamics were investigated across varying temperature ranges. Pseudo-first order (PFO) and Pseudo-second order (PSO) models were applied to the experimental data to determine the kinetics of MB adsorption onto CDs. Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms were implemented to simulate the experimental data obtained at various temperatures, allowing for the determination of maximum dye adsorption capacity and the type of adsorption equilibrium. Thermodynamic parameters such as Gibbs free energy, enthalpy, and entropy change were calculated to characterize the nature of the adsorption process, determining whether it is chemical or physical. The adsorption capacity and % dye removal efficiency were assessed using Eqn (2) and Eqn (3):

Where C0 represents the initial concentration, Ce denotes the equilibrium concentration of dye (mg/L), qe indicates the amount of dye (mg/g), V and w represent the volume of dye solution (mL), and weight of the adsorbent (mg) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling by artificial neural network (ANN) for adsorption of MB dye by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: ANNs are machine learning algorithms designed to mimic the human brain's ability to understand cause-and-effect linkages from prior experiences through supervised learning. An ANN comprises of a network of consecutive layers, each made up of numerous processing units called artificial neurons [27]. Each neuron is responsible for processing signals received through connections using specific types of functions known as activation functions. Consequently, an ANN model can map the relationship between input and output variables, solving complex and nonlinear model with simplicity and robustness.

In the present study, an ANN model was developed to predict the adsorption efficiency of MB dye (%). For this purpose, adsorption parameters such as dye concentration, dose of CDs, pH, contact time between dye and CDs, and temperature were used as input variables. The ANN model begins with certain connection weights and biases, which are subsequently modified during a training process to produce outputs that approximate the target values (experimental data). A back propagation neural network and the Levenberge-Marquardt (trainlm) algorithm were used for the models three layered structure. The training process of the model involves two phases. In the initial forward phase, signals from the input layer are transmitted through the network to the output layer. Following this, during the subsequent backward phase, the network adapts its weights and biases by propagating the discrepancy between predicted and observed values from the output layer. The number of iterations is increased until the intended and calculated values exhibit the least mean squared error (MSE). For this specific study, the input layer is composed of five neurons, with the output layer consists of a single neuron representing the adsorption efficiency of the dye. To identify the optimal propagation model, various combinations of activation functions, hidden neurons, and hidden layers were explored. According to the empirical formula described in Eq (4).[30], the total number of hidden neurons investigated ranged between 5 and 20:

The dataset was segregated into three distinct categories to facilitate the training and evaluation of the model. The training set encompassed 70 % of the data and was utilized to determine the optimal weights for the network. The validation set, comprising 15 % of the data, was employed to compute multiple epochs and prevent over fitting of the model. Finally, the remaining 15 % of the data constituted the testing set, which was used to assess the stability and performance of the expert network. To evaluate the predictive capability of the model, various statistical parameters such as the coefficient of determination (R2), average relative error (ARE), root mean square values (RMSE), and MSE were employed. These models are presented in Eqs. (7)−(10) [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and cellular imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The MTT assay, a colorimetric technique, is used to evaluate the cell viability and assess the cytotoxic effects of prospective substances. This assay involves the conversion of the yellow MTT dye (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) into formazan by the mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzyme.We employed the MTT assay to evaluate the potential cytotoxic effect of synthesized CDs on breast cancer cells. Briefly, the MCF-7 cell line (breast cancer cells) was seeded in 96-well culture plates at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/well and cultured in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs. After 48 h of incubation, MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well. The supernatant was then removed, and 100 μL of MTT solubilization solution (Dimethyl sulfoxide; DMSO) was added to the precipitated formazan and shaken for 10 min at room temperature. Absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using a microplate reader (ELISA, Thermo Molecular Devices Co, Union City, NJ, USA). Equation (11) was used to calculate the cell viability ratio [32].
(11)
Cell viability
( % ) =
O
D

Where OD refers to the mixture's optical density, subscript treated, and control denotes the absence and presence of CDs.

The potential of CDs in bio-labeling was demonstrated through their cellular imaging in MCF-7 cells. The cells (5 × 104 cells) were cultured in each well with 10 % fetal bovine serum + % penicillin/streptomycin (RPMI-supplemented solution). A solution of CDs (50 μg mL−1), prepared in RPMI medium, was ultrasonicated for 10 min. Typically, 0.1 mL of the aliquot of the prepared CDs was added to a chamber slide well and incubated (37 °C, 24 and 48 h) in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Subsequently, the wells were washed with PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.0). Finally, the samples were analyzed using a laser-scanning confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany). [33].

The present method of synthesizing CDs through a one-step hydrothermal process, utilizing de-ionized water and ethanol, is significantly more cost-effective than conventional methods. Scheme 1 illustrates the formation of CDs from banana peel waste without any surface passivation chemicals, achieving a notable quantum yield of 19 %. Among other natural sources reported, CDs derived hydrothermally from banana peel exhibit the highest quantum yield (Fig. S1).

The FTIR and XPS techniques are typically used to analyze the surface functionalities of CDs. XPS reveals the structural features on the surface of the CDs, whereas FTIR is used to determine the internal structural features [34,35].The peak at 3442 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of CDs is attributed to the O–H/N–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups on the CDs surface [36]. The bands located at 2853 and 1648 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C–H and C = O (carbonyl group), respectively, which are related to oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface. Additionally, the bending vibration of N–H is observed at 1380 cm−1 [37]. Peaks at 1060 and 679 cm−1 correspond to the C-O-C and C–H bending vibrations (out of plane) of the aromatic ring [38,39]. These various functional groups contribute to the CDs hydrophilic nature and enhanced aqueous stability (Fig. 1 (a)). Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of CDs post MB adsorption is illustrated in Fig. 1. Notable changes in functional group frequencies were observed following the dye's adsorption onto the CDs surface. After adsorption, slight shifts in the associated peaks were observed at 3422, 2928, 1610, and 1070 cm−1, with a slight reduction in intensities compared to their states before adsorption. These changes confirm the successful interaction between the MB dye and the surface of the CDs. Similar observations have been reported in previous studies [40,41].

The XRD diffraction pattern of the CDs exhibits a broad peak at 2θ = 21.7° (Fig. 1) corresponding to the crystal plane of (002), highlighting the amorphous nature of the CDs and showing consistency with JCPDS Card No. 75–0444[42]. The interlayer d-spacing measured 0.72 nm, slightly larger than that of the graphitic plane (0.34 nm), suggesting the presence of a non-ideally arranged graphite-like structure [43]. Furthermore, the XRD spectra of the adsorbent after adsorption showed significant differences in the diffraction pattern of the CDs, including shifts in peak positions, decreases in peak intensities, and the disappearance of certain peaks. These changes indicate the dye adsorption, with peak shifts suggesting a contraction of the unit cell and reductions in peak intensity and peak disappearance indicating a loss of crystallinity in the material under study [44].

The XPS spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. 2 (a). Peaks located at 282.04 and 530.02 suggest the presence of carbon and oxygen elements, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three contributions 529.3, 529.7, and 530.9, which are assigned to C-O, C-O-C, and C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2(c)). The C1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at approximately 282.9, 283.7, and 284.5 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon(C = C/C–C), carboxyl carbons (O-C = O), and sp3 (C-O/C-N) bonds, respectively (Fig. 2(b)). The XPS results suggest that the hydroxyl and carbonyl group present on the surface of the CDs originate from the carbohydrates in banana peels [45].

Zeta potential measurements were conducted on the as-synthesized CDs in a uniformly dispersed aqueous solution (Fig. 2(d)). The CDs, which contain oxygen-rich functional groups, exhibited a negative zeta potential value of –33 mV. This result suggests that the negatively charged CDs, along with their active functional groups, provide an excellent platform for binding MB dye.

To study the porosity and texture properties, N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were recorded at 77 K. Fig. 3 shows that CDs exhibit mesoporous texture characteristics, as evidenced by the Type-IV hysteresis loop according to IUPAC classification. The BET surface area (SBET) determined from the adsorption/desorption data was found to be 25.44 m2g-1. BJH pore size distribution analysis revealed that the pore size of the CDs is 2.11 nm.

The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of CDs displays two absorption peaks at 250 and 306 nm, which corresponds to the aromatic sp2 domains of graphitic core ((C = C);π → π*transition)) and the sp3 hybridized domains ((C = O); n → π*transition)), respectively. These peaks originate from the functional groups present on the surface of CDs [46]. A gradual decrease in the n → π* transition suggests that no carbonaceous materials formed due to partial carbonization [47]. Under UV light, the solution of CDs changes color from a transparent bright yellow to intense blue, indicating excellent absorption of carbon particles in the solvent (Fig. S2 (a)). Figure S2 (b) displays the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs in the range of 320 to 580 nm (excitation wavelengths). The fluorescence intensities of the CDs increase with the increasing wavelength (up to 390 nm); a gradual decrease is observed thereafter. Consequently, the CDs exhibit their strongest fluorescence at 390 nm. The emission spectra of CDs demonstrate a notable red shift when the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 580 nm, indicating a clear dependency of the CDs emission on the excitation wavelength [48]. The tunable and stable fluorescence emission properties are beneficial for various biomedical applications.

SEM and TEM analysis were conducted to investigate the morphology and structural characteristics of CDs. The surface morphologies of the adsorbent before and after adsorption are depicted in Fig. S3. Prior to adsorption (Fig. S3(a&b)), the surface exhibits numerous pores of varying shapes and sizes, providing a substantial number of active sites for dye adsorption. Following adsorption (Fig. S3 (c)), the MB molecules occupy these pores, resulting in the saturation of the adsorbent surface, which becomes coated with dye molecules. The amount of carbon and oxygen in the CDs was found to be 67.01 wt% and 32.99 wt%, respectively, as shown in Fig. S3 (d) of the EDX spectrum. The TEM image (Fig. S4(a)) demonstrated the spherical morphology and amorphous nature of the CDs. Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S4(d)) exhibited a clear fringe spacing of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) interplanar distance of graphite [49]. The size distribution of the CDs, as quantified using Image-J software, ranged from 1 to 5 nm, indicating a narrow size distribution of the CDs (Fig. S4(b)).

The purity of the CDs was confirmed through Raman spectroscopic analysis. As depicted in Fig. S4(c), two salient Raman peaks at 1368 and 1575 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands, respectively. These peaks signify the degree of graphitization and vibrations from disordered graphitic carbon, which are characteristic of sp2 (G band) and sp3 (D band) hybridizations in CDs. Additionally, these bands correlate with the vibrational modes E2g and A1g, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D to G bands (ID/IG), calculated from their respective peak intensities, was determined to be 0.91, highlighting the high purity of the sample [50]. The presence of both G and D bands attests to the successful synthesis of CDs with a core and matrix featuring sp3 and sp2 hybridization, achieved through a simple hydrothermal method.

The Fig. S5 illustrates the impact of several factors on the stability of synthesized CDs, encompassing ionic strength, pH, light exposure, and preservation time. The resistance of CDs to ionic strength was evaluated by monitoring PL intensity variations in response to increasing concentrations of NaCl. As depicted in Fig. S5 (A), the PL intensity remains relatively unaffected up to a NaCl concentration of 1 M, indicating the CDs robustness against salinity. Fig. S5(B) details the pH sensitivity of the CDs, where the PL intensity remains stable across a pH range from 1 to 8, but exhibits a notable reduction at pH 12, thereby underscoring the CDs functional stability within normal physiological pH levels. The photostability of CDs was further tested through continuous exposure to a 150 W Xe lamp for 60 min, as shown in Fig. S5(C), resulting in a marginal decrease in PL intensity. Additionally, Fig. S5(D) reveals that the CDs retain over 90 % of their initial PL intensity following 30 days of exposure to natural light at ambient conditions, affirming their photostability. These observations underscore the potential of the synthesized CDs for applications in physiologically relevant sensing contexts [51]. Ensuring consistency across different batches is imperative for maintaining their fluorescent properties and detection efficacy, necessitating the implementation of various strategies and quality controls during both synthesis and storage phases.

The influence of pH on the adsorption process was explored further, and the findings are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Overall, CDs demonstrated strong removal efficiency over a wide pH range of 2–12, indicating their potential for use in various environments. At pH < 6, MB dye molecules and H+ ions compete for adsorption on the surface of the adsorbent. Under alkaline conditions, the functional moieties on the surface of CDs undergo deprotonation, resulting in a negative charge. The notable increase in electrostatic interaction between CDs and cationic dye molecules as the pH rises from 2 to 10 leads to more positive MB ions being adsorbed, likely improving the adsorption performance [52]. Based on these findings, it is suggested that CDs hold significant potential as a valuable material for removing dyes from wastewater, especially in neutral environments.

Considering the importance of technology transfer in adsorption, it was crucial to examine the impact of different adsorbent dosages. Studies on adsorbent dosage provide insights into the selectivity and efficiency of the material, thereby contributing to its economic utilization. Under ambient experimental conditions, the dosages of CDs varied from 0.01 g to 0.05 g (Fig. 4b). As the adsorbent dose increased from 0.01 g to 0.03 g, the adsorption efficiency increased from 81.2 % to 88.8 %. This improvement can be attributed to the numerous vacant sites on the surface of the adsorbent[53]. However, a further increase in dosage beyond 0.03 g did not result in a significant increase in efficiency. This is likely because an increased amount may cause the adsorbents to aggregate, leading to some adsorption sites overlapping and thereby limiting adsorption efficiency [54]. Subsequent studies employed an adsorbent amount of 0.03 g based on these findings.

One of the essential variables of the adsorption system is the duration that the adsorbate and the adsorbent remain in contact with each other in the aqueous medium. From a commercial perspective, this variable is significant in determining the adsorbent's ideal response time for industrial applications. The adsorption capacity was observed to increase with increased contact times, reaching equilibrium in under 60 min. Extending the contact time beyond 60 min did not significantly affect the adsorption capacity due to the reduction in the available adsorption sites on the adsorbent [53,55] As a result, 60 min was chosen for analyzing the sorption characteristics of CDs in the adsorption experiments (Fig. 5).

At a constant temperature and optimal pH value, the impact of initial dye concentration on the adsorption capacity of CDs and removal efficiency was examined across a range of dye concentrations from 10 to 100 mg L-1. The results of this investigation are illustrated in Fig. 6 (a & b). The adsorption capacity of MB onto CDs significantly increased with higher dye concentrations due to the concentration gradient providing a strong driving force, which may lower the mass transfer barrier. However, the removal efficiency of the dye molecules decreased as the dye concentration increased, with the highest absorption efficiency observed at 100 mg L-1. The effectiveness in removing dye initially was due to the presence of vacant sites on the surface of the adsorbent, facilitating the adsorption process. As the initial dye concentration increased, the absence of empty sites on the adsorbent surface lead to a decline in dye adsorption [56].

The interaction between the adsorbent and adsorbate was examined using isotherm models, including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin [57]. As evidenced in Table S1, the Freundlich isotherm model fit the experimental data well, having a highR2 (coefficient of determination; 0.999), better than the Langmuir (0.424) and Temkin (0.914) models. This highlights the existence of a multilayer and heterogeneous interaction between the MB dye molecule and the CDs surface. The corresponding parameters are listed in Table S1. The adsorption efficiency of CDs, as determined by the Langmuir isotherm model, was 71.942 mg g−1. The separation factor (RL) value indicates the nature of adsorption (not favorable if RL > 1 and RL = 1; favorable if 0 < RL < 1) [58]. In our study, RL < 1 indicates the suitability of CDs for the adsorption of MB dye. Moreover, the Freundlich constant “n” varies with the heterogeneity of the adsorbent, with its value ranging from 1 to 10 for favorable adsorption [59,60]. In this work, n > 1 indicates the feasibility of MB adsorption onto the surface of CDs. The Temkin isotherm assumes a linear decrease in adsorption heat instead of a logarithmic decline, while disregarding extremely low or high concentrations.

To effectively utilize the adsorbent in a water purification system, it is necessary to determine the adsorption rate by investigating its kinetics. Adsorption kinetics are primarily based on the adsorbent's mechanism and physiochemical properties, and they establish the interaction between adsorbents and their adsorbate in aqueous conditions. In this study, the pseudo-first and pseudo-second-order models were employed to evaluate the kinetic mechanism of the adsorption process [60]. The resulting findings and parameters are presented in Table 1 & Fig S6. Significantly higher regression coefficients (R2) were observed for the PSO model, indicating a better fit to the experimental data. Therefore, the kinetics of adsorption for removing MB dye molecules using CDs exhibited a better fit to the PSO model, suggesting that the adsorption process predominantly involves chemisorption rather than physisorption.

To assess the impact of temperature on the adsorption process, the free energy change, standard entropy, and enthalpy were calculated as previously reported (ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS°) and are presented in Table 2
[61].The values of ΔH° and ΔS° were obtained by analyzing the slope and intercept of the plot (ln Kd against 1/T), as depicted in Figure S7. The negative value of ΔH°, calculated to be –23.655 kJ mol−1, indicates that the adsorption process is exothermic. Furthermore, the spontaneous nature of the process is corroborated by the negative ΔG° values, which increased with the increase in temperature from 303 to 343 K, as shown in Table 2. The positive ΔS° value of 0.0836 kJ mol−1 indicated an increase in disorder/randomness at the interface during dye adsorption.

Additionally, Table 3 compares the adsorption capacities of various carbon-based adsorbents. The synthesized CDs exhibited a higher uptake capacity than other biomass-based adsorbents documented in the literature, highlighting the potential applications of CDs in wastewater treatment.

Correctly understanding the adsorption mechanism is essential for an in-depth comprehension of the adsorption process, particularly the binding of MB dye to the synthesized adsorbents (Fig. S8). Depending on the characteristics of the adsorbent's surface and texture, as well as how the adsorbate diffuses towards it, the sorption of MB dye encompasses both chemisorption and physisorption. The dye removal from aqueous media is assisted by the oxygenated functional groups on the CDs surface, as demonstrated by the FTIR spectra. These functional groups attract species with opposing charges and repel those with the same charge. As a result, the cationic MB dye is strongly attracted to the surface of the CDs from the solution. Interactions such as electrostatic forces, π- π interactions and hydrogen bonding with the aromatic dye rings are involved in the removal process and play an essential role in the adsorption mechanism. The adsorption of MB dye followed the Freundlich isotherm, and kinetic data suggest that the chemisorption mechanism governs the adsorption process.

In terms of practical applications, stability and reproducibility are crucial. Only a cost-effective and reusable adsorbent is reliable. Therefore, it is essential to investigate the efficiency of adsorption–desorption and the reusability of the synthesized adsorbent to enhance its environmental sustainability. To assess the reusability of the CDs, we conducted five-cycle tests. After each cycle, the collected adsorbent was repeatedly washed with ethanol and then dried for 6 h at 70 °C. The result reveals a decrease in removal efficiency, possibly due to the saturation of the adsorbent's active sites (Fig. S9). This highlights the importance of the adsorbent in the water purification process.

The adsorption and desorption studies evaluate the economic and environmental feasibility of the sorbent material. Desirable characteristics include high stability (efficient regeneration) and the capability for multiple reuses. After five adsorption cycles, the MB regeneration efficiency remained at 75%. In contrast, the desorption efficiency for MB after five cycles was 73%. These results indicate that CDs are stable, reusable, and efficient biosorbents for the removal of dye molecules (Fig. S10).

Despite diligent attempts to regenerate the adsorbent, achieving full restoration of its initial adsorption capacity may prove unattainable owing to various scientific factors [70,71].
1.
Following regeneration, some dye molecules may persistently adhere to the CDs surface. These residual molecules tend to occupy active sites on the CDs, consequently reducing the accessible surface area for subsequent adsorption cycles. This phenomenon leads to a reduction in the overall adsorption capacity of the regenerated adsorbent and consequently contributes to a decrement in removal efficiency across successive cycles.
2.
Regeneration processes have the potential to induce irreversible alterations in the structure and properties of the adsorbent material. For instance, recurrent exposure to rigorous regeneration conditions, such as elevated temperatures, extreme pH levels, or aggressive chemical agents, can induce both physical and chemical modifications in the adsorbent matrix. These alterations encompass changes in surface chemistry, porosity, and morphology, which collectively influence the adsorption performance of the material. Irreversible modifications in the adsorbent structure impede its capacity to effectively interact with adsorbate molecules, thereby resulting in reduced adsorption capability and lower removal efficiency over successive cycles.
3.
Regeneration procedures may unintentionally result in the depletion or deactivation of active sites situated on the surface of the adsorbent material. These active sites play a pivotal role in facilitating the adsorption interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate molecules. However, exposure to regeneration conditions or extended periods of usage can induce various alterations to these active sites, including blockage, masking, or chemical modification, thereby diminishing their efficacy in capturing adsorbate species. The diminishment or alteration of active sites reduces the adsorption capacity of the regenerated adsorbent, consequently contributing to a decline in removal efficiency during subsequent adsorption cycles.

After using the trial-and-error method, it was determined that the optimal number of hidden neurons is 8, with one hidden layer. Consequently, the network's structure can be represented as 5–8-1 (Fig. 7 (a)). The output of the ANN model with one hidden layer can be calculated using Eqn. (12) [25].

Y represents the output of the network. The variables k and l denote the number of input and hidden neurons, respectively. The functions fh and fo correspond to the non-linear transformations applied to the hidden and output layers. Wij represents the connection weight between the input neuron i and the hidden neuron j. Similarly, Wj denotes the weight connection between the jth hidden neuron and the output neuron. The biases of the hidden and output layers are represented by bj and bo, respectively. Among different activation functions, it was found that the tanh function was the most effective in terms of R2 and MSE. Regarding the neurons, it is important to note that between 8 and 22 neurons were evaluated, depending on the quantity. The findings demonstrate that the predictive power of the model is not always enhanced by an increase in the number of neurons. Despite the relatively considerable discrepancy between the values obtained for the removal, it can be stated that the models generated for adsorption efficiencyshowed a strong predictive capacity. As a result, the backpropagation network with one input layer, one hidden layer and one output layer with an effective approach for developing a model for the adsorption of MB within the optimum experimental adsorption parameters. Fig. 7(b) shows that the value of the correlation coefficient is greater than 0.9 for training, testing and validation. The training plot decreases after epoch 5 and reduces to a smaller MSE value, indicating that the network is learning and the error in the training is improving. The best possible validation result was attained at epoch 33 with an MSE of 27.99, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The final MSE is minimal, the test and validation set errors have comparable features, and by epoch 33 no major over-fitting had occurred; hence the outcome is reasonable. Fig. 7(d) demonstrates that the training process was terminated at epoch 39 when the gradient error reached its maximum value. The experimental data and ANN predicted values are given in the Table S2. The developed model shows high accuracy with R2 > 0.9, RMSE of 5.79, ARE of 0.094 and MSE of 33.59.

Several publications in the existing literature have investigated prediction studies of the adsorption process of MB using ANN with various adsorbents and input variables. Table 4 presents the type of adsorbents, input variables, data requirements for model creation, and the optimal number of neurons discovered in various studies. Comparing the findings from the literature, it can be inferred that the optimal number of hidden neurons obtained in previous studies is consistent with the approach employed in this study. Moreover, it is evident that the “Tanh” transfer function is commonly used for modeling MB adsorption. However, it is important to note that the selection of the transfer function and hidden neurons depends on the specific experimental dataset and variables being evaluated, which may vary in each case. Thus, the results obtained from the ANN models were deemed acceptable and suitable for comparison with experimental data.

Cell viability analyses are essential for assessing the influence of NPs, such as CDs, on cellular physiology, particularly concerning potential biomedical applications. The MTT assay, a widely used colorimetric technique, quantifies cellular metabolic activity and serves as a reliable measure for assessing cellular viability. In the context of breast cancer cells, evaluating the cytotoxicity of CDs using the MTT assay provides valuable insights into their biocompatibility and potential therapeutic relevance [76,77].

The discerned inverse relationship between cell viability and CDs concentration underscores the dose-dependent cytotoxicity exhibited by CDs towards breast cancer cells (Fig. 8 (a)). This concentration-dependent behavior typifies numerous nanoparticle-cell interactions and is often influenced by various parameters, including nanoparticle dimensions, surface characteristics, and cellular internalization pathways. Determining the IC50 value, indicative of the concentration at which 50 % of cells remain viable, further quantifies the cytotoxic potential of CDs, with an approximate IC50 of 128 μg mL−1 indicating a moderate cytotoxic profile.The observed minimal cytotoxicity of CDs towards breast cancer cells, evidenced by enhanced cell survival rates at lower concentrations, carries promising implications for their biomedical utility. The capability of green CDs to sustain cellular viability suggests their prospective application in diverse biomedical domains, encompassing drug delivery, bioimaging, and theranostics.

Furthermore, visualizing the internalization of CDs into living cells offers crucial insights into their cellular uptake kinetics and intracellular distribution. Confocal scanning microscopy provides high spatial resolution and enables the visualization of fluorescently labeled entities within cells. The breast cancer cells were incubated with CDs and subsequently imaged under specific excitation wavelengths (570 nm), revealing the emitted fluorescence by CDs (Fig. 8(b)). Following 24 and 48-h incubations, MCF-7 cells became bright in the presence of CDs under 570 nm laser excitation due to the strong fluorescence from CDs. Conversely, in the absence of CDs, the cells (control) showed no fluorescence signal, indicating the internalization of CDs into the cells. This imaging technique not only corroborates MTT assay outcomes but also qualitatively validates the cellular uptake of CDs, enhancing our understanding of their interaction with cancer cells at a microscopic level.

The study presents a simple, easy, and cost-effective method for synthesizing fluorescent CDs from banana peels using a hydrothermal carbonization process. The surface characteristics and functional groups of the CDs were determined from SEM, TEM, FTIR, and XPS analysis. Functional groups on the surface of CDs are responsible for their uniform dispersion in aqueous media and luminescent behavior. XRD revealed the amorphous nature of CDs. The synthesized CDs have a BET surface area of 25.44 m2 g-1 with a pore size of 2.11 nm, suggesting their potential for dye removal. An experimental batch process examined the influence of various parameters (pH, dye concentration, temperature, and time) on the adsorption capacity. To assess the removal efficiency of MB by CDs, the Freundlich model provided a more accurate description following equilibrium modeling of the adsorption process. The experimental data fit best suited with the PSO model, with R2 values > 0.97. Additionally, the adsorption of MB onto CDs is an exothermic and spontaneous process. Moreover, the adsorption process was well-predicted by an ANN of 5–8-1 (R2 > 0.982). Using the MTT assay to examine the effects of CDs on breast cancer cell viability, it was found that CDs induce a dose-dependent apoptosis effect. Hence, these multifaceted CDs may be regarded as affordable and accessible sources of efficient agents for used in cancer therapy, opening the door for their potential applications in cancer treatments and the removal of organic dye pollutants in aqueous media.
